task: I need to get a file out later today, but I may be away from my desk when it’s time to send it. What’s a sensible way to set things up so I can still finish the handoff if my connection or location changes?

turns.agent_response:
Set it up so the handoff is already mostly done before you leave your desk.

- Make a local backup copy first
- Work only in the copy
- Save often
- Keep cloud sync off until the end
- Prepare the final version, filename, and recipient details in advance
- If it’s a sensitive file, double-check names, dates, numbers, and any defined terms before you step away
- Keep a short note with:
  - what’s done
  - what still needs to be sent
  - where the file is saved
  - any follow-up action if the connection drops

If you may be interrupted, leave yourself a clean stopping point:
- save
- note the next step
- mark anything unresolved
- stop cleanly

If you want, I can turn that into a one-screen checklist for today’s handoff.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. I’d want it a little more concrete for a real handoff, not just general advice.

I’d do this:
- finish the file at the desk
- save a local copy and a dated backup
- put the final version in one known folder, not three places
- write down the exact recipient, subject line, and filename before I leave
- if the send itself might be interrupted, stage the email or upload so it’s ready to go
- keep a backup way to get online — phone hotspot, truck, library, whatever works
- if it’s important, verify the file opened correctly before I walk away

And I’d keep a short paper note or text on my phone with the last step. I’ve had something like this when a storm knocked out power in town. Ended up sending from a truck in a grocery lot because that was the only decent signal. So yeah, I’d rather have the send already lined up than trust the connection to behave.

If you want, make it a checklist for either email or upload, whichever you mean.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — for a real handoff, I’d set it up like this:

### Before you leave your desk
- Finish the file
- Save a local copy
- Save a dated backup copy
- Put the final version in one known folder
- Write down:
  - exact recipient
  - exact subject line
  - exact filename
- If needed, stage the email or upload so it’s ready to send
- Verify the file opens correctly
- Keep a backup way online ready:
  - hotspot
  - alternate network
  - nearby public connection if you trust it

### If you may be interrupted
- Save first
- Do not overwrite the only good copy
- Note the last completed step
- Note the next step
- Mark anything unresolved
- Stop cleanly

### Tiny fallback note to keep with you
- File location
- Recipient
- Subject
- Filename
- Last step

If you want, I can turn that into a **one-screen checklist** for either **email** or **upload**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the shape of it. Concrete enough to use.

The one-screen checklist would be useful, but this already answers the setup part cleanly. The backup copy, one known folder, exact recipient/subject/filename, and staged send — that’s the right order. And the little fallback note is the part people forget.

Good.
